name: Van Gogh's Montmartre Apartment (54 Rue Lepic)
price: Free (exterior only — private residence with plaque)
reviewSummary: The unassuming apartment building where Vincent lived with his brother Theo from 1886 to 1888 — the two years that turned him from a painter of dark Dutch scenes into the master of color. A plaque marks the third-floor flat; the view he painted from the window still exists.
googleReviewSummary: Van Gogh pilgrims call it a quiet, moving stop on the walk up Rue Lepic toward the windmills he painted.
localTip: Stand across the street to spot the plaque, then walk two minutes uphill to the Moulin de la Galette, which Vincent painted repeatedly — you're literally retracing his daily walk.
highlight: Vincent's actual home — he painted over 200 works in his two Paris years here
story: Vincent moved into the third floor of this building in 1886 to live with his art-dealer brother Theo, and Paris rewired him completely. He arrived painting dark peasant scenes and left two years later painting in the explosive color that made him immortal — he discovered the Impressionists, Japanese prints, and absinthe, more or less in that order. From the window of their apartment he painted the rooftops of Paris, and on this street he painted the windmills just uphill. Almost nobody on this busy market street realizes they're walking past the most important address in his artistic life.

name: Auvers-sur-Oise — Van Gogh's Final Village
address: Auberge Ravoux, 52 Rue du Général de Gaulle, 95430 Auvers-sur-Oise
price: Village free; Auberge Ravoux room ~10€; train ~6€ each way (1h from Gare du Nord/Saint-Lazare)
reviewSummary: The village where Van Gogh spent his last 70 days in 1890 — and painted 70+ canvases, one a day. You can stand in his tiny preserved attic room at the Auberge Ravoux, see the actual church and wheatfields he painted, and visit his grave beside Theo's, covered in ivy.
googleReviewSummary: Visitors describe it as deeply moving and remarkably unchanged — the painted scenes are instantly recognizable in the real streets and fields.
localTip: Go on a weekend in summer when the direct seasonal train runs from Gare du Nord; do the loop — auberge, church, wheatfields, cemetery — with the free walking map. The room is closed Mondays-Tuesdays.
highlight: His preserved attic room, the church he painted, and his grave next to Theo's
story: Vincent came to this quiet village in May 1890 to be treated by Doctor Gachet, and in seventy days produced one of the most furious creative bursts in art history — more than seventy paintings, including The Church at Auvers and Wheatfield with Crows. He died here in July, in a tiny attic room at the Auberge Ravoux that has been kept empty and unchanged ever since — by tradition, no one has slept in it since. He's buried in the village cemetery beside the wheatfields, next to Theo, who died six months after him; the brothers' graves are covered by a single blanket of ivy taken from Doctor Gachet's garden.

name: Giverny — Monet's Gardens & House
address: Fondation Claude Monet, 84 Rue Claude Monet, 27620 Giverny
price: 14.50€ adults; train to Vernon ~10-15€ (45 min from Saint-Lazare) + shuttle. Open April-November
reviewSummary: Monet's pink house and the gardens he designed and painted for 40 years — the actual water-lily pond and green Japanese bridge from the world's most beloved paintings. Astonishing in every season it's open; tour groups peak midday.
googleReviewSummary: Visitors call walking into the water-lily pond 'stepping inside a painting'; the recurring advice is to arrive at opening or after 4pm to dodge the coaches.
localTip: Pair it with the Orangerie in Paris — see the real pond in the morning, then the giant Water Lilies panels it became in the afternoon. Wisteria on the Japanese bridge peaks mid-May.
highlight: The real water-lily pond and Japanese bridge from the paintings
story: Claude Monet spotted Giverny from a train window in 1883, rented a pink farmhouse, and spent the next forty-three years turning its grounds into his greatest artwork. He diverted a river branch to dig the water-lily pond, imported Japanese prints' worth of bamboo and wisteria, and employed six gardeners — one of whose only jobs included dusting the lilies and rowing out to wash the pads. The two hundred and fifty water-lily canvases he painted here ended up in museums on every continent, including the great oval rooms of the Orangerie, which he donated to France as a monument to peace.

name: Musée des Arts Décoratifs (MAD)
reviewSummary: France's great design museum, in a wing of the Louvre palace: 1.5 million objects spanning medieval tapestries to Art Nouveau interiors, iconic chairs, posters and blockbuster fashion exhibitions (Dior, Schiaparelli, Iris van Herpen have all broken records here).
googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise world-class fashion and design shows and gorgeous period rooms, far less crowded than the Louvre next door.
localTip: Check what fashion exhibition is on — they're the best in Paris and sell out weekends; the Art Nouveau and Art Deco period rooms on the upper floors are the permanent-collection secret.
highlight: The best fashion & design exhibitions in Paris + jewel-box Art Nouveau rooms
story: This museum was founded in the 1870s by craftsmen and collectors determined to prove that everyday objects — chairs, posters, teapots, dresses — deserve the same reverence as paintings. It lives in the Marsan wing of the Louvre palace and owns over a million objects, from Marie Antoinette's furniture to 1980s Memphis design. Its fashion exhibitions have become legendary: the Christian Dior retrospective was so popular people queued four hours, making it one of the most visited shows in French history.

name: Musée Carnavalet — History of Paris
reviewSummary: The story of Paris itself, told through 3,900 objects in two joined Marais mansions — Revolution relics, Proust's actual bedroom, Belle Époque shop signs, and Marie Antoinette's slipper. Completely free, with one of the loveliest courtyard gardens in the Marais.
googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it the best free museum in Paris after its renovation — beautifully curated and never overwhelming.
localTip: Go straight to the Revolution rooms (the original Declaration of the Rights of Man drafts) and Proust's cork-lined bedroom; the garden café is a perfect Marais pause.
highlight: Proust's actual bedroom and the French Revolution's relics — free
story: Carnavalet is the oldest museum of the city of Paris, housed in a Renaissance mansion where the Marquise de Sévigné wrote her famous letters in the 1670s. It hoards the city's memory: the keys to the Bastille, Napoleon's picnic case, the cork-lined bedroom where Proust wrote In Search of Lost Time, and entire rescued interiors — a Belle Époque jewelry shop, an Art Nouveau café — reassembled room by room inside. And the whole thing is free, which may be the most generous gift Paris gives its visitors.

name: Musée de la Vie Romantique
reviewSummary: A green-shuttered 1830 villa at the end of a cobbled lane, once the salon of painter Ary Scheffer — Chopin, Liszt, Delacroix and George Sand all passed through. Now a free museum of the Romantic era with a rose-garden greenhouse tearoom that's one of the most charming in Paris.
googleReviewSummary: Reviewers adore the secret-garden setting and tearoom; the small collection is charming rather than essential.
localTip: Come for the garden tearoom under the climbing roses (spring-summer) as much as the museum; pair with a walk through the SoPi 'New Athens' quarter of artists' villas.
highlight: George Sand's memorabilia + the prettiest garden tearoom in Paris — free
story: In the 1830s this quarter was nicknamed 'the New Athens' because so many Romantic artists settled here, and this villa was its beating heart: painter Ary Scheffer held Friday salons where Chopin played, Liszt argued, and George Sand — the cigar-smoking novelist who wore men's suits and scandalized Paris — held court. The museum keeps her jewelry, manuscripts, and even a cast of her lover Chopin's delicate left hand. The rose-covered greenhouse in the garden serves tea, and on a quiet morning the nineteenth century feels about ten minutes away.

name: Musée Gustave Moreau
reviewSummary: The symbolist painter's house-museum, left exactly as he designed it — thousands of jewel-toned mythological paintings floor-to-ceiling around the most beautiful spiral staircase in any Paris museum. A cult favorite of artists and almost empty on weekdays.
googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call the double-helix staircase and crammed studio walls breathtaking — a hidden gem that feels like entering the artist's mind.
localTip: The famous cast-iron spiral staircase between the two studio floors is the photo; weekday mornings you may have it alone.
highlight: That spiral staircase — the most beautiful in Paris — amid 25,000 works
story: Gustave Moreau, the painter of glittering myths and femmes fatales, spent his final years converting his family house into his own museum — he designed the hanging, the staircase, everything — then left it to France on the condition that nothing ever change. Nothing has. His student Henri Matisse called him a liberating teacher; the Surrealists worshipped this place, and André Breton said he discovered beauty here. Nearly 25,000 works crowd the walls of two soaring studios connected by a cast-iron spiral staircase so lovely it has its own following on Instagram.

name: Musée National Eugène Delacroix
reviewSummary: The Romantic master's last apartment and garden studio on the magical Place de Furstemberg — small, intimate, and built around the private garden he tended while painting the murals of Saint-Sulpice nearby.
googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the peaceful hidden garden and intimate scale; it's a 45-minute visit, best as part of a Saint-Germain stroll.
localTip: Your Louvre ticket gets you in free the same day; come late afternoon when the garden catches the light, then apéro at La Palette two streets over.
highlight: The painter's private garden studio — free with a Louvre ticket
story: Delacroix — the painter of Liberty Leading the People, the most famous French painting after the Mona Lisa — moved here in 1857, old and ill, specifically to be near his final great commission: the murals of Saint-Sulpice church, a short walk away. He had a studio built in the garden and wrote in his journal that waking to its greenery made him happy every single day. Picasso and other admirers later fought to save the apartment from demolition, founding the museum — making this small garden one painter's gift handed down by another.
